An old chow had lived in a hut there for many years, and fossicked on the old diggings, and one day he was found dead in the hut, and the Government gave some one a pound to bury him. She read the names on the stone, and said it was a nice stone, and asked questions about how the children were found and brought here. She seemed quite sensible, and very cool about it. But when he got her home she was back on the fairy idea again. He tried another day, but it was no use; so then he let it be. I think it's better as it is. The roads were good, the weather grand-no chance of it raining, and I had a spare tarpaulin if it did — I would only camp out one night ; so I decided to take Jim home with me. Jim was turning three then, and he was a cure. He was so old-fashioned that he used to frighten me sometimes — I'd almost think that there was some- thing supernatural about him ; though, of course, I never took any notice of that rot about some chil- dren being too old-fashioned to live.
